CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a logical flow (Intro → Related Work → Method → Training → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states the main contribution early (intro and the opening of Section 3 describe the DualNet/Collaborative Deep Embedding). Notation is mostly consistent and well-defined (e.g. R, ui, vj and equations (1)–(4) are clear), and the method description (Sections 3.1–3.2) is easy to follow. Presentation issues are minor but notable: figures/tables are referenced (Figure 1, Tables 1–3) but are not shown here, and the manuscript contains duplicated/repeated citation fragments and some formatting/noise near the end (multiple repeated sentences and citation lines) that reduce polish and may confuse readers.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clearly articulated new architecture: jointly trained dual deep encoders for items and users (user encoding replaces the linear CF transform with g(r;Wu) in Eq.(3)), together with multi-level aligned embeddings and a multi-level branching design, and a dual-mini-batch training procedure (Secs.3–4). It explicitly differentiates itself from closest prior work (CDL and item-only deep encoders) and demonstrates improved out-of-matrix generalization (Sec.3 comparison, Sec.5 experiments). However, the work builds on well-studied recommendation formulations (WMF, SDAE/CDL) and combines/extensions of existing components rather than introducing a fundamentally new problem or theoretical framework, so the novelty is notable but not radical.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Overall the technical approach is sound and the main claims are supported by experiments comparing against reasonable baselines (WMF and CDL) on three datasets, including in-matrix and out-matrix evaluations. Minor issues: a small algebraic typo in Eq.(2) (the right-hand side should be V Ci ri rather than V ri), limited discussion of optimization/scalability and hyperparameter choices for the nonconvex joint training, and no reported statistical significance of the reported gains. These issues do not invalidate the core technical contribution or the empirical findings.
